cards.back: A build configuration is a collection of project settings that determines how your IDE builds your project. It includes compiler, linker, and other tool settings.

cards.text: The two most common build configurations are {{c1::Debug}} (for development and debugging) and {{c2::Release}} (for shipping to users).

cards.front: What is the Debug build configuration optimized for?
cards.back: Debug builds are optimized for debugging. They include debug symbols, disable optimizations (for easier debugging), and may include extra error checking. They compile faster but run slower.

cards.front: What is the Release build configuration optimized for?
cards.back: Release builds are optimized for performance and small size. They enable compiler optimizations, strip debug symbols, and produce fast executables for end users. They compile slower but run faster.

cards.concept: Why you should develop using Debug builds
cards.explanation:
Debug builds are essential during development:
  • Include debugging information for breakpoints and variable inspection
  • Disable optimizations that make debugging confusing
  • Add runtime checks for errors (bounds checking, assertions)
  • Faster compilation times for rapid iteration
  • Error messages point to actual source code lines
Only switch to Release when performance testing or shipping.
examples: Debug: see variable values at any point during execution
Debug: step through code line by line
Release: optimizations can reorder/eliminate code, confusing debugger

cards.text: Debug builds include {{c1::debug symbols}} and {{c2::disable optimizations}}, while Release builds {{c3::enable optimizations}} and {{c4::strip debug symbols}}.

cards.front: Why are Debug builds typically larger than Release builds?
cards.back: Debug builds include debugging symbols (symbol tables, line number information) and unoptimized code. Release builds strip symbols and apply optimizations that reduce code size.

cards.front: How much faster can Release builds run compared to Debug builds?
cards.back: Release builds can be significantly faster - often 2-10x faster than Debug builds depending on the code. This is due to compiler optimizations.

cards.text: Use {{c1::Debug}} configuration while actively developing and debugging, then switch to {{c2::Release}} configuration for performance testing and distribution.

cards.front: Why might a bug appear in a Release build but not in a Debug build?
cards.back: Compiler optimizations in Release builds can expose undefined behavior or timing-dependent bugs (race conditions, uninitialized variables) that don't appear in Debug builds.

cards.front: What should you do before distributing your program to users?
cards.back: Build and thoroughly test the Release configuration. Never ship Debug builds - they're slower, larger, and may contain debugging artifacts.

cards.explanation:
Professional development uses configurations strategically:
  1. Develop and debug in Debug configuration
  2. Fix all bugs and warnings
  3. Switch to Release and rebuild
  4. Test performance and functionality
  5. Fix any Release-only bugs
  6. Ship the Release build
Some teams add configurations like RelWithDebInfo (optimized but with debug symbols).
